serving.description: 1 serving — stir-fry with fried egg, no rice (~280g)
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard Thai restaurant serving of pad kra pao consists of approximately 280g of minced pork stir-fried with holy basil and topped with a fried egg, served without rice, providing approximately 400 kcal.

nutrient_source.title: Thailand Food Composition Tables — Pad Kra Pao (Stir-fried Holy Basil Minced Pork with Egg)
nutrient_source.organization: Institute of Nutrition, Mahidol University (INMU), Thailand Food Composition Tables
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables used as primary source for per-100g nutrient values for pad kra pao (stir-fried holy basil minced pork with egg). INMU is an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional food composition database for Southeast Asian dishes. Values represent the composite dish including minced pork, holy basil, garlic, chilli, fish sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, sugar, vegetable oil, and fried egg. Monounsaturated and polyunsaturated fat breakdown not reported in INMU for this composite dish; set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Mixed Stir-fried Dishes — Analogous Estimate Based on University of Sydney GI Database; stir-fried minced pork with vegetables and sauces
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Glycemic Index Foundation)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for pad kra pao in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI ~30 is estimated based on the macronutrient profile of the stir-fry: the dish is predominantly protein (9.3g/100g) and fat (9.3g/100g) with low available carbohydrate (4.3g/100g). The small carbohydrate load derives from oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and sugar — all present in small quantities. Analogous reference: stir-fried meat and vegetable dishes with sauce (GI 30–40, University of Sydney GI Database). GL per 280g serving = (30 × 12g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 3.6, rounded to 3.3 per input source. Confidence: 0.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content — approximately 1,200mg sodium per 280g serving, primarily from fish sauce (~600–700mg) and oyster sauce (~300mg)
hypertension.guidance: Hypertensive patients should treat pad kra pao as a high-sodium dish (1,200mg Na per serving, representing 52–60% of the 2,000mg daily limit); request reduced fish sauce (50% reduction saves ~400mg Na), substitute reduced-sodium oyster sauce where available, and avoid adding additional condiments at the table.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very low glycemic load (GL ~3.3 per serving) from the stir-fry alone; high protein (26g per serving) supports satiety and glycaemic control
type2_diabetes.guidance: Pad kra pao stir-fry (without rice) is an excellent choice for Type 2 Diabetes management due to its very low GL of ~3.3, high protein content of ~26g per serving, and moderate fat; however, the full meal GL rises substantially to ~42 when served with 200g jasmine rice, making rice portion control the primary dietary intervention.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium (1,200mg per serving) combined with moderate saturated fat (~7g per serving) and dietary cholesterol (~224mg per serving including fried egg)
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease patients, pad kra pao presents a dual concern: very high sodium (1,200mg per serving) which elevates blood pressure risk, and moderate saturated fat and cholesterol from pork mince and fried egg (~7g SFA, ~224mg cholesterol per serving); request reduced fish sauce and consider omitting the fried egg to reduce cholesterol load by approximately 185mg.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sodium (1,200mg per serving) is a concern for RA patients on NSAIDs; moderate saturated fat from pork mince
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients on NSAIDs should be aware of the high sodium content (1,200mg per serving) which may exacerbate fluid retention; the holy basil eugenol has theoretical COX-inhibiting anti-inflammatory properties but culinary amounts are insufficient for clinical effect.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Pad Kra Pao (ผัดกระเพราหมูสับ) is the most emblematic Thai street food dish — stir-fried minced pork with Thai holy basil (Ocimum tenuiflorum, กระเพรา krapao), cooked at furiously high wok heat with garlic, bird's eye chillies, fish sauce, oyster sauce, dark soy sauce, and sugar, topped with a crispy fried egg. The dish is served as a single-plate meal, typically over jasmine rice. Per 280g serving (stir-fry + fried egg, no rice): approximately 400 kcal, 26g protein, 26g fat, 12g carbohydrate, and 1,200mg sodium. The sodium is very high — fish sauce contributes ~600–700mg and oyster sauce ~300mg per serving. GI ~30 (low), GL ~3.3 per serving (stir-fry only) — an excellent glycaemic profile for T2DM when eaten without rice. The full meal GL with 200g jasmine rice rises to ~42. SINGAPORE NOTE: Holy basil (peppery, clove-like eugenol flavour) is frequently substituted with sweet basil (โหรพา horapha) in Singapore Thai restaurants — no macronutrient difference. This is the single most-ordered Thai dish at Singapore Thai restaurants (Jai Thai, Nakhon Kitchen, Pad Thai Fah). Source: INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables. Confidence: HIGH.

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced from INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables, an approved FAO/INFOODS-affiliated regional food composition database for Southeast Asian dishes. The dish is a canonical Thai preparation with well-documented composition. All core macros, secondary nutrients, and micronutrients are reported. GI is an analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database methodology, confidence 0.9.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite Thai stir-fry dish is not reported in the INMU Thailand Food Composition Tables as of 2026. water_g set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish not reported in INMU source. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible/zero per INMU source for this preparation. SINGAPORE BASIL NOTE: Holy basil (กระเพรา krapao, Ocimum tenuiflorum — peppery, clove-like eugenol flavour) is frequently substituted with sweet basil (โหรพา horapha) in Singapore Thai restaurants; no macronutrient difference between the two basil varieties at culinary quantities. The 280g serving weight includes: minced pork 150g + holy basil 20g + garlic, chilli, sauces, oil + fried egg. Sodium per serving (~1,200mg) is calculated as 428.6mg/100g × 2.8 = ~1,200mg. The full meal with 200g jasmine rice adds approximately 58g carbohydrate and raises GL to ~42. Halal status: not applicable — dish contains pork.
